item: "Reviewed the Astrolabe plandoc for w-mo3depkw (Spider Dispatch Gating via spider.follows) against the codebase to check reality-fit and help Sean pick on the open decisions. Caught the stacked-branch topology: plan reads like fiction against main (linkMeanings/semanticMeaning/status-as-lifecycle) but is faithful against its own working tree (draft-mo3detn9-71ae606a), which has the two prior substrate commissions (Link-Substrate Rename, Status Convention) stacked as unmerged commits 62451cc/75de531/f56beee. Verified the plan's API/type/line-number claims against the draft branch — all accurate. Walked Sean through the 22 decisions: D10–D22 pre-empted or auto-decided with `selected`, D1–D9 open. D1 (shape of status.spider) is the only one deliberately surfaced for patron review; I recommended `enriched-on-stuck` (planner's pick), arguing the future Oculus gate-column (parked c-mo301yp9) can cheaply one-hop re-walk outbound links without needing status.spider written on every poll tick. D2–D9 all planner-auto-decided under razor/three-defaults — endorsed each as-is with brief rationale per row. Flagged as positive evidence for planner-quality: the razor codification (b003c39, f641362) is producing correctly-calibrated auto-decide discrimination — 8 of 9 auto-decided under three-defaults and the 1 deliberate surface is exactly the inter-component persisted-data-shape case the razor targets. No clicks opened; no files modified during the substantive review."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: cd39734f-144a-4a4e-a3cd-1f7607f73faa
    date: 2026-04-18
    item: "Design conversation on rig composition → fleshed out the multi-rig click subtree → dispatched a tactical-bridge brief as w-mo3pwsts. Opening question was Sean's 'should composable rigs via { include: rig_template_id } be a thing' with a concrete use case: collapse brief → planner → mandate → implementation into one rig. I first proposed writ-spawning as the composition primitive (parent rig spawns child writ for the sub-rig). Sean pushed back: two writs for one obligation breaks blocked_by dependency-gating semantics — the whole point of the exercise. Reframed onto the existing multi-rig design (archived quest bodies w-mnsxbn8m cross-rig data flow + w-mnsxa94f brief→mandate collapse, which Sean pointed me at): one writ hosts sequenced rigs, typed workspace artifacts carry data between them, composition is rig-sequence-on-writ not rig-template inclusion. Concluded c-mo3nwx31 with that reframe. Fleshed out c-mo1mqeti's sub-clicks with 19 decomposed children sourced from the archived quest 'Open' sections (three-meanings, cross-rig data flow, rig template generation, brief-collapse, plus two top-level opens). Sean then asked for a tactical-bridge click under dependency gating (c-mo3ibjl0) — ship a specialized combined-rig today to buy correct brief-level gating without waiting on the full refactor. Drafted the brief with two iteration forks from Sean: (1) my initial 'guild.json is authoritative for mappings' framing was backward — Sean wanted plugin-level defaults to be the zero-config source of truth, guild.json overrides as optional customization; (2) extend scope to move the mandate path (default rig template + mandate → default mapping) into Spider's supportKit as plugin defaults, not just Astrolabe's brief path. Final brief shape: Astrolabe registers + defaults the combined rig for briefs; Spider registers + defaults the implementation rig for mandates; guild.json shrinks accordingly. One writ throughout the combined rig — no phantom mandate, which is the dependency-gating fix. Dispatched complexity is not yet set; Sean skipped volunteering one and I didn't push. Note for methodology: I did not do a commission-log sweep at session start — Sean's first message was a substantive design question, which per the startup heuristics means skip the sweep and engage."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: c8e045e8-eb9e-432b-8706-b54d07a17bdb
    date: 2026-04-18
    item: "Guild-boot triage → SQLite data migration for two schema renames → Spider rig-detail SSE removal + uniform transcript polling + live elapsed ticker. Session started with `nsg start --foreground` failing: reconciler blew up on a writ with `phase: undefined`. Sean identified the missing migration (status→phase rename had code-side updates but no data-side migration). Ran the SQL migration in-place against /workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db after backup: 49 writ rows migrated (status→phase), then a second migration layer on 108 link rows (type→label, add kind:null, drop semanticMeaning) after the next failure revealed the link rename also needed a data pass. Dropped seven stale JSON-path indexes referencing the old field names. Opened two top-level clicks as follow-on tracking: c-mo3qlbgh (reconciler boot robustness — should loading a malformed writ block the whole reconciler?) and c-mo3qlc0z (schema-to-data migration coordination — low-priority since only one guild exists). Second thread: Sean flagged rig-mo3icdxj had stopped live-refresh entirely rather than 'keeping refresh, but stopping the flicker.' First diagnosis incorrectly concluded the SSE path was preserved; Sean corrected with concrete observation (F5 revealed chunks of missed transcript). Second diagnosis identified the actual bug — removing the 2 s wholesale rebuild simultaneously killed the implicit SSE reconnect. I proposed patching SSE reconnect; Sean overrode with the architecturally correct move: 'there is no SSE path that is valid' because animator.subscribeToSession only works in-process and sessions run detached. Implemented the fix directly at Sean's explicit direction ('make the changes yourself, no dispatch here'): deleted the /api/spider/session-stream route and its test block; removed EventSource + all SSE state/handlers from spider.js; added startSessionTranscriptPoll / stopSessionTranscriptPoll / fetchAndRenderTranscript (2 s poll of /api/spider/session-transcript, driven from updateEngineDetail so every rig poll retargets cleanly via dedupe). Per Sean's additional ask, replaced the static 'running…' placeholder in the Elapsed field with a 1 s local ticker (startElapsedTimer / stopElapsedTimer) that calls formatElapsed(startedAt, now()) each tick for a visible live pulse without a server roundtrip. Rewrote the spider-ui.test.ts SSE describe into an SSE-removal regression guard plus new describes for transcript polling and the elapsed ticker; all 611 Spider tests green. Typecheck has a pre-existing unrelated error (missing @shardworks/loom-apparatus module) — confirmed pre-existing via stash. Commissionable in principle (contained UI refactor with clear acceptance criteria) but Sean directed direct implementation so no brief was drafted."

Verified the plan's API/type/line-number claims against the draft branch — all accurate. Walked Sean through the 22 decisions: D10–D22 pre-empted or auto-decided with `selected`, D1–D9 open. D1 (shape of status.spider) is the only one deliberately surfaced for patron review; I recommended `enriched-on-stuck` (planner's pick), arguing the future Oculus gate-column (parked c-mo301yp9) can cheaply one-hop re-walk outbound links without needing status.spider written on every poll tick. D2–D9 all planner-auto-decided under razor/three-defaults — endorsed each as-is with brief rationale per row. Flagged as positive evidence for planner-quality: the razor codification (b003c39, f641362) is producing correctly-calibrated auto-decide discrimination — 8 of 9 auto-decided under three-defaults and the 1 deliberate surface is exactly the inter-component persisted-data-shape case the razor targets. No clicks opened; no files modified during the substantive review."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: cd39734f-144a-4a4e-a3cd-1f7607f73faa
    date: 2026-04-18
    item: "Design conversation on rig composition → fleshed out the multi-rig click subtree → dispatched a tactical-bridge brief as w-mo3pwsts. Opening question was Sean's 'should composable rigs via { include: rig_template_id } be a thing' with a concrete use case: collapse brief → planner → mandate → implementation into one rig. I first proposed writ-spawning as the composition primitive (parent rig spawns child writ for the sub-rig). Sean pushed back: two writs for one obligation breaks blocked_by dependency-gating semantics — the whole point of the exercise. Reframed onto the existing multi-rig design (archived quest bodies w-mnsxbn8m cross-rig data flow + w-mnsxa94f brief→mandate collapse, which Sean pointed me at): one writ hosts sequenced rigs, typed workspace artifacts carry data between them, composition is rig-sequence-on-writ not rig-template inclusion. Concluded c-mo3nwx31 with that reframe. Fleshed out c-mo1mqeti's sub-clicks with 19 decomposed children sourced from the archived quest 'Open' sections (three-meanings, cross-rig data flow, rig template generation, brief-collapse, plus two top-level opens). Sean then asked for a tactical-bridge click under dependency gating (c-mo3ibjl0) — ship a specialized combined-rig today to buy correct brief-level gating without waiting on the full refactor. Drafted the brief with two iteration forks from Sean: (1) my initial 'guild.json is authoritative for mappings' framing was backward — Sean wanted plugin-level defaults to be the zero-config source of truth, guild.json overrides as optional customization; (2) extend scope to move the mandate path (default rig template + mandate → default mapping) into Spider's supportKit as plugin defaults, not just Astrolabe's brief path. Final brief shape: Astrolabe registers + defaults the combined rig for briefs; Spider registers + defaults the implementation rig for mandates; guild.json shrinks accordingly. One writ throughout the combined rig — no phantom mandate, which is the dependency-gating fix. Dispatched complexity is not yet set; Sean skipped volunteering one and I didn't push. Note for methodology: I did not do a commission-log sweep at session start — Sean's first message was a substantive design question, which per the startup heuristics means skip the sweep and engage."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: c8e045e8-eb9e-432b-8706-b54d07a17bdb
    date: 2026-04-18
    item: "Guild-boot triage → SQLite data migration for two schema renames → Spider rig-detail SSE removal + uniform transcript polling + live elapsed ticker. Session started with `nsg start --foreground` failing: reconciler blew up on a writ with `phase: undefined`. Sean identified the missing migration (status→phase rename had code-side updates but no data-side migration). Ran the SQL migration in-place against /workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db after backup: 49 writ rows migrated (status→phase), then a second migration layer on 108 link rows (type→label, add kind:null, drop semanticMeaning) after the next failure revealed the link rename also needed a data pass. Dropped seven stale JSON-path indexes referencing the old field names. Opened two top-level clicks as follow-on tracking: c-mo3qlbgh (reconciler boot robustness — should loading a malformed writ block the whole reconciler?) and c-mo3qlc0z (schema-to-data migration coordination — low-priority since only one guild exists). Second thread: Sean flagged rig-mo3icdxj had stopped live-refresh entirely rather than 'keeping refresh, but stopping the flicker.' First diagnosis incorrectly concluded the SSE path was preserved; Sean corrected with concrete observation (F5 revealed chunks of missed transcript). Second diagnosis identified the actual bug — removing the 2 s wholesale rebuild simultaneously killed the implicit SSE reconnect. I proposed patching SSE reconnect; Sean overrode with the architecturally correct move: 'there is no SSE path that is valid' because animator.subscribeToSession only works in-process and sessions run detached. Implemented the fix directly at Sean's explicit direction ('make the changes yourself, no dispatch here'): deleted the /api/spider/session-stream route and its test block; removed EventSource + all SSE state/handlers from spider.js; added startSessionTranscriptPoll / stopSessionTranscriptPoll / fetchAndRenderTranscript (2 s poll of /api/spider/session-transcript, driven from updateEngineDetail so every rig poll retargets cleanly via dedupe). Per Sean's additional ask, replaced the static 'running…' placeholder in the Elapsed field with a 1 s local ticker (startElapsedTimer / stopElapsedTimer) that calls formatElapsed(startedAt, now()) each tick for a visible live pulse without a server roundtrip. Rewrote the spider-ui.test.ts SSE describe into an SSE-removal regression guard plus new describes for transcript polling and the elapsed ticker; all 611 Spider tests green. Typecheck has a pre-existing unrelated error (missing @shardworks/loom-apparatus module) — confirmed pre-existing via stash. Commissionable in principle (contained UI refactor with clear acceptance criteria) but Sean directed direct implementation so no brief was drafted."
    commissionable: true

content.content:
1	# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
2	#
3	# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
4	# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
5	# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
6	# picture of how work is routed in the system.
7	#
8	# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
9	# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
10	#
11	# Fields:
12	#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
13	#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
14	#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
15	#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission
16	#   justification  — why it can't be commissioned (required when commissionable is false)
17	#
18	# Justifications (required when commissionable: false):
19	#   interactive     — required real-time iteration with Sean
20	#   sanctum         — sanctum/experiment/meta work outside the commission boundary
21	#   decisional      — output was a decision, not an artifact
22	
23	entries:
24	  - session: 8d1d17b8-8db3-4a6a-8382-4df6c73b594e
25	    date: 2026-04-04
26	    item: "Delete legacy quality-review scripts, update all references to instrument-review.sh"
27	    commissionable: false
28	    justification: sanctum
29	
30	  - session: 8d1d17b8-8db3-4a6a-8382-4df6c73b594e

#

#   decisional      — output was a decision, not an artifact

input.command:
git add .claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md experiments/data/coco-log.yaml && GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
clicks skill: add 'Enumerated decisions → enumerated children' trigger

Diagnosed in-session: when analysis enumerates N items with per-item
evaluations, those evaluations are latent sub-clicks and should be
lifted into children rather than collapsed into the parent's
conclusion. Adds a drafting-time recognition trigger so the pattern
fires automatically without prompting.

Session: 0d7b3898-c814-4d7b-bbf2-62abce104471
EOF
)" && git status

content.content:
1	# Guild Vocabulary — Emerging Concepts
2	
3	Latent vocabulary for the guild metaphor: concepts that are still cooking, not yet needed for bootstrap, or awaiting design decisions. Everything here is compatible with the foundational metaphor in `/workspace/nexus/docs/guild-metaphor.md` but hasn't been promoted to foundational status yet.
4	
5	This document is a **tome** (in its own metaphor) — reference material to consult, not work-in-progress. When opening a new quest, scan this file for related terms and cross-link any matches in the quest's References section. That manual habit is the discovery mechanism: the vocabulary doesn't surface itself; Coco surfaces it by remembering to look.
6	
7	**Active vocabulary bookmark quests** — terms with feature-shaped gaps that have earned their own parked-quest entries:
8	
9	- **Coinmaster / Purse / Tithe** → cost tracking & token budget allocation (`w-mnszznt5-66d0ec7464bc`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*
10	- **Petition** → first-class internal commissions (`w-mnszzo8h-42137bda6681`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*
11	- **Vigil** → background monitoring of in-flight commissions (`w-mnszzon4-731bd9827d05`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*
12	
13	Other terms in this tome remain latent — they live here until they earn a bookmark or get absorbed into an active inquiry.
14	
15	## Roles (not yet foundational)
16	
17	| Role | Function | Status |
18	|------|----------|--------|
19	| **Guildmaster** | Top-level decision maker. Interfaces with the patron on behalf of the guild. Determines priorities and allocates resources. | Clear concept, not needed until guild has enough activity to require coordination. |
20	| **Coinmaster** | Tracks AI token balances and expenditures. Provides cost visibility and may participate in resource allocation decisions (e.g., which petitions to grant). | Real need, not blocking anything yet. |
21	| **Oracle** | Answers questions about code and system design. Loosely defined — a consultative role invoked when agents need understanding of existing systems. | Useful but not in the critical path. |
22	
23	## Work (not yet foundational)
24	
25	### Petition
26	
27	A commission originating from within the guild rather than from the patron. Petitions are requests to build or improve guild resources — paying tech debt, upgrading tooling, refactoring internal systems. The intent is that leadership evaluates petitions and decides which to grant, balancing cost against value. Petitions use the same commission infrastructure but are internally motivated.
28	
29	## Communication
30	
31	### Inbox
32	
33	Each guild member has an inbox — a queue where messages are delivered for the member to act on. When a message arrives, it is passed to the member's agent in context, and the member does whatever their role dictates. Inboxes are the primary mechanism for asynchronous coordination between guild entities. (Format and mechanics TBD.)
34	
35	## Records & History
36	
37	### Stories (working term)
38	
39	Logs, transcripts, and metadata produced by guild activities. Session logs, commission records, trial outcomes, sage advice, cost reports — the narrative record of what the guild did, why, and how it went. The term is provisional; candidates include *chronicles*, *annals*, or *scrolls*. The concept is stable even if the name isn't.
40	
41	## Open Questions
42	
43	- **Council of Sages protocol:** When the Master Sage convenes the council, what does that look like mechanically? Parallel consultation? Sequential? Does the Master Sage synthesize, or does each sage contribute independently?
44	- **Stories naming:** The working term "stories" doesn't quite land. Need to find the right guild-flavored word for the system's operational records.
45	- **Inbox mechanics:** What does the inbox look like? File-based queue? Database rows? How does an engine know when to deliver? Polling? Event-driven?
46	- **Seal mechanics:** The concept of a seal (a member's mark of authorship) is clear, but the format is undefined. Git authorship serves as an interim stand-in. See Coco's suggestions below for the full concept.
47	- ~~**"Summons" vs "Summon":** Resolved — "summon" removed from foundational metaphor (engine renamed to "compose", later to "manifest"). "Summons" is free for its natural use as an urgent, targeted request for a member's attention.~~
48	
49	---
50	
51	## Coco's Suggestions
52	
53	Everything below is Coco riffing. Unvetted, unfiltered, organized loosely by theme. Take what resonates, discard the rest.
54	
55	### Authority & Governance
56	
57	**Charter** — the founding document of a guild house. A charter defines the house's purpose, its workshops, its standing members, and any constraints on its autonomy. When a new house is created, it gets a charter. The charter is what makes a house *legitimate* — without one, it's just a directory with aspirations. System mapping: the configuration/manifest file that defines a house's scope, repos, and member assignments.
58	
59	**Writ** — a formal authorization to act. Distinct from a commission (which describes *what* to do), a writ grants *permission* to do it. A commission might be posted but not yet authorized — awaiting budget approval from the Coinmaster, or strategic sign-off from the Guildmaster. The writ is what turns a posted commission into a dispatchable one. This could be the missing piece in the petition flow: a house submits a petition, and the Guildmaster issues a writ to authorize it. System mapping: an approval gate between `posted` and `ready` status.
60	
61	**Seal** — a member's mark of authorship on their work. When an artificer completes a commission, the work bears their seal. When a sage produces advice, the advice is sealed. Seals create provenance — you can always trace *who* produced *what*. System mapping: author metadata on commits, commission records, and advice objects. Already partially exists (git author), but formalizing it in the guild model makes it intentional rather than incidental.
62	
63	**Edict** — a directive from leadership that applies across the entire guild. Not a commission — an edict doesn't produce deliverables. It changes *how the guild operates*. System mapping: system-wide configuration changes, policy updates. Edicts vs. commissions is the difference between "build X" and "from now on, do Y." *(Refined and specified in the v2 architecture — see `.scratch/nexus-architecture-v2.md`.)*
64	
65	**Decree** — like an edict, but scoped to a single house. A housemaster can issue decrees for their house without involving the guildmaster. System mapping: house-level configuration overrides.
66	
67	**Oath** — a binding commitment made by a member. "I will never modify files outside my commission scope." "I will always run tests before sealing my work." Oaths are identity-level — the difference between an edict ("do X on this commission") and an oath ("I always do X, on every commission, it's who I am"). *(Refined and specified in the v2 architecture as a composition component — see `.scratch/nexus-architecture-v2.md`.)*
68	
69	### Knowledge & Learning
70	
71	**Tome** — a large, authoritative knowledge document. The guild metaphor doc itself is a tome. The project philosophy is a tome. A tome is *reference material* — not instructions (those are scrolls), not records (those are stories/chronicles), but accumulated knowledge meant to be consulted. System mapping: docs that agents are instructed to read for context. The key insight: tomes are written *for agents to read*, not for humans. They're part of the system's knowledge architecture.
72	
73	**Scroll** — a small, portable instruction document. A member's custom instructions are a scroll. A commission spec is a scroll. Scrolls are *actionable* — they tell you what to do, not what to know. The distinction from tomes: you *study* a tome, you *follow* a scroll. System mapping: agent instruction files, commission specs, operational directives.
74	
75	**Lore** — accumulated institutional knowledge that isn't written down anywhere specific. The things the guild "just knows" from experience. An artificer who has completed 50 commissions in a workshop has lore about that codebase — patterns, pitfalls, architectural decisions. System mapping: this is the hard one. Lore is what's currently lost between agent sessions. If the register tracked not just instructions but *experience summaries* per anima, that's lore. This is where the Academy gets really interesting — an instructor could distill a member's lore into teachable knowledge.
76	
77	**Rune** — a small, reusable fragment of instruction or knowledge. Not a full scroll — more like a macro or a snippet. "Always use async/await, never raw promises." "This repo uses vitest, not jest." Runes can be composed into scrolls, attached to workshops, or stamped onto commission records. System mapping: reusable prompt fragments, repo-specific conventions, composable instruction modules.
78	
79	### Economics & Resources
80	
81	**Purse** — a token budget allocated to a specific commission or member. The Coinmaster manages the guild's overall treasury; purses are the parceled-out allocations. A commission gets a purse when dispatched. If the purse runs dry, the commission must stop or request more funds. System mapping: token budget limits per agent invocation. This is a real operational need — runaway agents are expensive. The purse makes cost containment part of the metaphor rather than an afterthought.
82	
83	**Tithe** — a portion of resources contributed back to the guild from each commission. Not every token spent on a commission goes to implementation — some overhead goes to sage consultation, engine operation, record-keeping. The tithe is that overhead, made visible. System mapping: tracking the "cost of coordination" separately from the "cost of implementation." Useful for understanding system efficiency.
84	
85	**Bounty** — a reward or incentive attached to a commission. In the current system this is metaphorical, but it could become real: a bounty could represent priority level, resource allocation, or quality expectations. A high-bounty commission gets a senior artificer and a full sage consultation. A low-bounty commission gets dispatched with minimal ceremony. System mapping: commission priority/tier system that affects dispatch decisions.
86	
87	**Levy** — a mandatory contribution from houses to the guild. Each house might owe a certain amount of capacity to guild-wide commissions (patron-originated work) before they can pursue their own petitions. System mapping: resource allocation policy — ensuring patron work takes priority over internally-generated work.
88	
89	### Quality & Verification
90	
91	**Assay** — an examination of completed work to determine its quality. When an artificer completes a commission, the work is assayed before it's accepted. Does it work? Does it meet the spec? Does it break anything? System mapping: automated testing, CI checks, integration verification. The assayer could be a role — or it could be an engine (mechanical quality checks) with an Oracle consulted for judgment calls.
92	
93	**Hallmark** — a quality stamp applied to work that has passed assay. Hallmarked work is trusted; unhallmarked work is provisional. A workshop might refuse unhallmarked contributions. System mapping: CI passing, code review approval, merge criteria. The hallmark is what gates completed work entering the workshop permanently.
94	
95	**Trial by Craft** — a demonstration of skill required before a member is trusted with certain work. An apprentice artificer might need to complete trial commissions in a sandboxed environment before being dispatched to real workshops. System mapping: agent evaluation/qualification — running test commissions to verify a member's capabilities before giving them production access. This ties into the Academy: the Academy trains, the trial proves.
96	
97	### Lifecycle & Ceremony
98	
99	**Muster** — the act of assembling members for a commission. Before dispatch, the system musters the required members: checks the roster, verifies availability, confirms the sage is ready, ensures the artificer is active. System mapping: the pre-dispatch validation step. Currently implicit in `send`, but formalizing it as "muster" makes it a named, debuggable phase.
100	
101	**Vigil** — a period of watching and waiting. After a commission is dispatched, someone (or something) keeps vigil — monitoring progress, watching for failures, waiting for completion. System mapping: the background monitoring that checks commission status, detects failures, and triggers alerts. Currently a manual status check. An engine could keep vigil automatically.
102	
103	**Rite of Naming** — the ceremony by which a new anima is called into being and given their identity. Not just "add to database" — the naming is when the spirit receives its name and seal, becoming a distinct presence in the guild. The rite could include an initial training session at the Academy (aspirant phase), a trial by craft, and formal induction to the roster as an active anima.
104	
105	**Requiem** — the process of retiring an anima. Not dissolution — the anima's record persists in the register forever, their seals remain on their work, their lore is preserved. But the spirit passes to rest: `retired` state, no longer manifested. System mapping: a formal process that includes archiving the anima's current instructions and recording why they were retired.
106	
107	### Communication & Coordination
108	
109	**Herald** — a specialized engine (or role?) that announces events across the guild. Commission completed. New member inducted. Petition granted. House chartered. The herald doesn't decide anything — it broadcasts. System mapping: an event/notification system. Webhooks, log events, Slack notifications, whatever — the herald is the abstraction over "tell everyone something happened."
110	
111	**Summons** — a formal request for a specific member's attention. Different from a message in the inbox — a summons is urgent and targeted. "The Oracle is summoned to examine the authentication module." System mapping: a high-priority, synchronous invocation of a specific member, as opposed to async inbox delivery. *(Naming conflict resolved — "summon" removed from foundational metaphor, engine renamed to "compose", later to "manifest.")*
112	
113	**Parley** — a structured conversation between two or more members. When the Master Sage convenes the Council, that's a parley. When an artificer encounters something unexpected and needs to consult the Oracle mid-commission, that's a parley. System mapping: agent-to-agent communication within a commission lifecycle. This is the multi-agent coordination primitive — how do two AI agents actually talk to each other?
114	
115	**Dispatch** — already used informally, but worth naming explicitly. A dispatch is the act of sending a member on a commission. It includes the muster (validation), the writ (authorization), and the actual invocation. System mapping: the send command, but decomposed into named phases.
116	
117	### Spatial & Territorial
118	
119	**Ward** — a protective boundary around a workshop or house. Wards define what members can and cannot do within a space. "No force-pushes to main." "No modifications to the auth module without Oracle consultation." System mapping: branch protection rules, path-based permissions, pre-commit hooks, CODEOWNERS — all the guardrails that prevent members from damaging work.
120	
121	**Commons** — external resources the guild draws on but does not build or maintain. GitHub, AWS accounts, credentials for external services, docker sockets, API keys. The guild operates on the commons; it does not own them. Registered in the guildhall as metadata; credentials and secrets live outside the guildhall. Animas are told which commons are available at dispatch.
122	
123	### The Weird Stuff (speculative, possibly dumb, possibly brilliant)
124	
125	**Familiar** — what if, instead of being a synonym for engine, a familiar is an engine *bound to a specific member*? Every member could have a personal familiar — a set of mechanical scripts tailored to that member's role and habits. The artificer's familiar manages their git workflow. The sage's familiar gathers codebase context before consultation. System mapping: per-role automation that wraps the agent invocation with role-specific pre/post processing.
126	
127	**Enchantment** — a persistent augmentation applied to a workshop or tool. An enchanted workshop might have automatic linting, type-checking, or test-running on every commit. An enchanted CLI tool might have built-in telemetry. Enchantments are set-and-forget improvements that make everything in their scope better. System mapping: CI/CD pipelines, git hooks, automated quality tooling — but framed as *enhancements to the artifact* rather than external processes.
128	
129	**Prophecy** — a prediction about future work, made by the Oracle or Master Sage. "If we continue on this architecture, we will hit scaling problems in the auth module within 3 commissions." "The test suite will become the bottleneck before the code does." Prophecies are speculative and may be wrong, but they inform prioritization. System mapping: AI-generated technical forecasting. Feed an Oracle the full codebase and ask "what's going to break next?" Use prophecies to generate petitions proactively.
130	
131	**Ritual** — a prescribed sequence of actions performed by engines and members in coordination. A deployment is a ritual. A release is a ritual. Onboarding a new workshop is a ritual. Rituals are repeatable, documented, and partially mechanical (engine steps) and partially intelligent (member steps). System mapping: runbooks, playbooks, CI/CD pipelines with manual gates — any multi-step process that blends automation with judgment.
132	
133	**Pilgrimage** — a commission undertaken not for deliverables, but for knowledge. Send an artificer (or sage) to explore an unfamiliar codebase, library, or technology — not to build anything, but to return with lore. The lore is then available to the guild for future commissions. System mapping: exploratory spikes, research tasks, "go figure out how this library works and write up what you learn." The output is a tome or lore entry, not code.
134	
135	---
136	
137	## Weak Concepts (kept for reference, minimal utility)
138	
139	These concepts have been considered and found to have limited functional value. They're preserved here in case future needs give them a purpose, but they don't currently earn a place in the metaphor or architecture.
140	
141	**School** — the identity and disposition produced by a curriculum. What animas "come from," what outcomes accumulate against. In theory, querying "how did Thomson-school animas perform?" is a school-level question. In practice, the curriculum name already carries this identity — `training/curricula/thomson/v2.md` is a Thomson artifact without needing "school" as a separate concept. School is a human-legible grouping label, not a system entity. No engine, implement, or Ledger table references "school" as a distinct thing. If the guild ever needs to track performance or reputation across curriculum versions, the concept might earn its place. Until then, it's just another name for "curriculum name."
142

# Guild Vocabulary — Emerging Concepts

Latent vocabulary for the guild metaphor: concepts that are still cooking, not yet needed for bootstrap, or awaiting design decisions. Everything here is compatible with the foundational metaphor in `/workspace/nexus/docs/guild-metaphor.md` but hasn't been promoted to foundational status yet.

This document is a **tome** (in its own metaphor) — reference material to consult, not work-in-progress. When opening a new quest, scan this file for related terms and cross-link any matches in the quest's References section. That manual habit is the discovery mechanism: the vocabulary doesn't surface itself; Coco surfaces it by remembering to look.

**Active vocabulary bookmark quests** — terms with feature-shaped gaps that have earned their own parked-quest entries:

- **Coinmaster / Purse / Tithe** → cost tracking & token budget allocation (`w-mnszznt5-66d0ec7464bc`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*
- **Petition** → first-class internal commissions (`w-mnszzo8h-42137bda6681`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*
- **Vigil** → background monitoring of in-flight commissions (`w-mnszzon4-731bd9827d05`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*

Other terms in this tome remain latent — they live here until they earn a bookmark or get absorbed into an active inquiry.

## Roles (not yet foundational)

| Role | Function | Status |
|------|----------|--------|
| **Guildmaster** | Top-level decision maker. Interfaces with the patron on behalf of the guild. Determines priorities and allocates resources. | Clear concept, not needed until guild has enough activity to require coordination. |
| **Coinmaster** | Tracks AI token balances and expenditures. Provides cost visibility and may participate in resource allocation decisions (e.g., which petitions to grant). | Real need, not blocking anything yet. |
| **Oracle** | Answers questions about code and system design. Loosely defined — a consultative role invoked when agents need understanding of existing systems. | Useful but not in the critical path. |

## Work (not yet foundational)

### Petition

A commission originating from within the guild rather than from the patron. Petitions are requests to build or improve guild resources — paying tech debt, upgrading tooling, refactoring internal systems. The intent is that leadership evaluates petitions and decides which to grant, balancing cost against value. Petitions use the same commission infrastructure but are internally motivated.

## Communication

### Inbox

Each guild member has an inbox — a queue where messages are delivered for the member to act on. When a message arrives, it is passed to the member's agent in context, and the member does whatever their role dictates. Inboxes are the primary mechanism for asynchronous coordination between guild entities. (Format and mechanics TBD.)

## Records & History

### Stories (working term)

Logs, transcripts, and metadata produced by guild activities. Session logs, commission records, trial outcomes, sage advice, cost reports — the narrative record of what the guild did, why, and how it went. The term is provisional; candidates include *chronicles*, *annals*, or *scrolls*. The concept is stable even if the name isn't.

## Open Questions

- **Council of Sages protocol:** When the Master Sage convenes the council, what does that look like mechanically? Parallel consultation? Sequential? Does the Master Sage synthesize, or does each sage contribute independently?
- **Stories naming:** The working term "stories" doesn't quite land. Need to find the right guild-flavored word for the system's operational records.
- **Inbox mechanics:** What does the inbox look like? File-based queue? Database rows? How does an engine know when to deliver? Polling? Event-driven?
- **Seal mechanics:** The concept of a seal (a member's mark of authorship) is clear, but the format is undefined. Git authorship serves as an interim stand-in. See Coco's suggestions below for the full concept.
- ~~**"Summons" vs "Summon":** Resolved — "summon" removed from foundational metaphor (engine renamed to "compose", later to "manifest"). "Summons" is free for its natural use as an urgent, targeted request for a member's attention.~~

---

## Coco's Suggestions

Everything below is Coco riffing. Unvetted, unfiltered, organized loosely by theme. Take what resonates, discard the rest.

### Authority & Governance

The sage's familiar gathers codebase context before consultation. System mapping: per-role automation that wraps the agent invocation with role-specific pre/post processing.

**Enchantment** — a persistent augmentation applied to a workshop or tool. An enchanted workshop might have automatic linting, type-checking, or test-running on every commit. An enchanted CLI tool might have built-in telemetry. Enchantments are set-and-forget improvements that make everything in their scope better. System mapping: CI/CD pipelines, git hooks, automated quality tooling — but framed as *enhancements to the artifact* rather than external processes.

**Prophecy** — a prediction about future work, made by the Oracle or Master Sage. "If we continue on this architecture, we will hit scaling problems in the auth module within 3 commissions." "The test suite will become the bottleneck before the code does." Prophecies are speculative and may be wrong, but they inform prioritization. System mapping: AI-generated technical forecasting. Feed an Oracle the full codebase and ask "what's going to break next?" Use prophecies to generate petitions proactively.

**Ritual** — a prescribed sequence of actions performed by engines and members in coordination. A deployment is a ritual. A release is a ritual. Onboarding a new workshop is a ritual. Rituals are repeatable, documented, and partially mechanical (engine steps) and partially intelligent (member steps). System mapping: runbooks, playbooks, CI/CD pipelines with manual gates — any multi-step process that blends automation with judgment.

**Pilgrimage** — a commission undertaken not for deliverables, but for knowledge. Send an artificer (or sage) to explore an unfamiliar codebase, library, or technology — not to build anything, but to return with lore. The lore is then available to the guild for future commissions. System mapping: exploratory spikes, research tasks, "go figure out how this library works and write up what you learn." The output is a tome or lore entry, not code.

---

## Weak Concepts (kept for reference, minimal utility)

These concepts have been considered and found to have limited functional value. They're preserved here in case future needs give them a purpose, but they don't currently earn a place in the metaphor or architecture.

**School** — the identity and disposition produced by a curriculum. What animas "come from," what outcomes accumulate against. In theory, querying "how did Thomson-school animas perform?" is a school-level question. In practice, the curriculum name already carries this identity — `training/curricula/thomson/v2.md` is a Thomson artifact without needing "school" as a separate concept. School is a human-legible grouping label, not a system entity. No engine, implement, or Ledger table references "school" as a distinct thing. If the guild ever needs to track performance or reputation across curriculum versions, the concept might earn its place. Until then, it's just another name for "curriculum name."

| **Oracle** | Answers questions about code and system design. Loosely defined — a consultative role invoked when agents need understanding of existing systems. | Useful but not in the critical path. |

### Distiller

A pre-plan role that consumes a brief and produces a **clarified-feature-spec** that becomes the input contract for the reader-analyst phase of Astrolabe. The distiller resolves product-layer ambiguity (what the feature is, who it's for, how it behaves from the user's perspective) without touching implementation-layer questions, which remain the analyst's domain. Three techniques: **extract** from the brief text, **extrapolate** from surrounding context, **interview** (typically the Patron Anima) to fill gaps. Output is a six-section structured document (Summary, Behaviors, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, Edge Cases, Open Questions) with provenance tagging on every item.

Previously called BA/PO (Business Analyst / Product Owner) during initial design; renamed to Distiller because the role's essence is distilling a messy brief into a clarified, machine-parseable spec.

System mapping: a new pre-plan stage in the Astrolabe pipeline (click subtree under `c-mo3qj676`).

item: "Guild-boot triage → SQLite data migration for two schema renames → Spider rig-detail SSE removal + uniform transcript polling + live elapsed ticker. Session started with `nsg start --foreground` failing: reconciler blew up on a writ with `phase: undefined`. Sean identified the missing migration (status→phase rename had code-side updates but no data-side migration). Ran the SQL migration in-place against /workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db after backup: 49 writ rows migrated (status→phase), then a second migration layer on 108 link rows (type→label, add kind:null, drop semanticMeaning) after the next failure revealed the link rename also needed a data pass. Dropped seven stale JSON-path indexes referencing the old field names. Opened two top-level clicks as follow-on tracking: c-mo3qlbgh (reconciler boot robustness — should loading a malformed writ block the whole reconciler?) and c-mo3qlc0z (schema-to-data migration coordination — low-priority since only one guild exists). Second thread: Sean flagged rig-mo3icdxj had stopped live-refresh entirely rather than 'keeping refresh, but stopping the flicker.' First diagnosis incorrectly concluded the SSE path was preserved; Sean corrected with concrete observation (F5 revealed chunks of missed transcript). Second diagnosis identified the actual bug — removing the 2 s wholesale rebuild simultaneously killed the implicit SSE reconnect. I proposed patching SSE reconnect; Sean overrode with the architecturally correct move: 'there is no SSE path that is valid' because animator.subscribeToSession only works in-process and sessions run detached. Implemented the fix directly at Sean's explicit direction ('make the changes yourself, no dispatch here'): deleted the /api/spider/session-stream route and its test block; removed EventSource + all SSE state/handlers from spider.js; added startSessionTranscriptPoll / stopSessionTranscriptPoll / fetchAndRenderTranscript (2 s poll of /api/spider/session-transcript, driven from updateEngineDetail so every rig poll retargets cleanly via dedupe). Per Sean's additional ask, replaced the static 'running…' placeholder in the Elapsed field with a 1 s local ticker (startElapsedTimer / stopElapsedTimer) that calls formatElapsed(startedAt, now()) each tick for a visible live pulse without a server roundtrip. Rewrote the spider-ui.test.ts SSE describe into an SSE-removal regression guard plus new describes for transcript polling and the elapsed ticker; all 611 Spider tests green. Typecheck has a pre-existing unrelated error (missing @shardworks/loom-apparatus module) — confirmed pre-existing via stash. Commissionable in principle (contained UI refactor with clear acceptance criteria) but Sean directed direct implementation so no brief was drafted."
    commissionable: true

    item: "Vision doc + extensive click work under the autonomous-operation umbrella. Drafted .scratch/vision-planner-quality.md — named the four planner-quality umbrellas (fidelity, decisional machinery, scope rethink, verification) and positioned observations→commissions as a neighbor under autonomy's self-improvement, not a planner-quality umbrella. Created c-mo3hinjt (system self-improvement) under autonomy and reparented c-mo39ro9x under it. Sean pushed back on my initial phase ordering (fidelity first, decisional machinery last) — reframed with decisional machinery as centerpiece and fidelity as 'quick confidence-builder, don't over-invest.' Did analytical work on the 9 decisional-machinery children, grouped into three layers (evidence gatherers, deliberation mechanisms, meta/routing), recommended Patron Anima as first entry point. Sean picked Patron Anima. Opened c-mo3o0euk (MVP scoping) with 6 children (output shape, pipeline placement, taste corpus, singleton-vs-family, self-uncertainty, MVP cut). Settled output shape via c-mo3o28hb: confirm/override/fill-in verdict mandatory, pick from offered options only (no custom — that's the human's escape hatch), confidence low/med/high mandatory from day one, applied to every decision, override-rate tracked as a first-class instrument with override×confidence giving two orthogonal quality signals. Pivoted on pipeline-placement after Sean questioned why we preserved the 'batch patron input at end' shape — reframed Patron Anima as potentially present throughout planning. Opened pre-plan interview (c-mo3phvuw) and mid-plan consultation (c-mo3phwj6 parked). Sean then reframed pre-plan further: not 'resolve brief ambiguity' but 'BA/PO talking to product-owner about features and external behavior, nothing code-level.' ### c-mo3o0euk-054bbde3ae5f [live]

#### c-mo3o28hb-a5fa7b0a95f9 [concluded]
> What does Patron Anima output — challenge questions, veto votes, annotations on proposed decisions, or something else?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Patron Anima emits, per decision:
- **verdict**: confirm / override / fill-in (mandatory; no abstain, no surface-flip)
- **selection**: picked from analyst's offered options only (no custom — human patron keeps that escape hatch; anima is deliberately more constrained for measurable, type-safe output)
- **confidence**: low / med / high (mandatory from day one — don't treat as optional or it'll get skipped)

Applied to every decision in the plan (both surfaced and auto-decided). Output shape is identical to existing patron-feedback shape, so it plugs into decision-review engine without new plumbing.

**Override rate tracked as a first-class planner-quality instrument.** Override × confidence gives two quasi-orthogonal signals:
- high override + high confidence → planner is weak on taste-relevant decisions
- high override + low confidence → anima is flailing
- high confirm + low confidence → anima doesn't know patron well enough
- high confirm + high confidence → system is working

**Low-confidence handling default: don't raise to patron.** Flip to 'raise if confidence < X' only if metrics show problems.
Created: 2026-04-18T01:36:39.791Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T01:52:09.724Z

Conclusion: Corpus is the input set to authoring a guild-specific patron role file — system prompt prose installed via Astrolabe config — not a runtime retrieval pipeline. MVP authoring sources: concluded click conclusions (Sean-approved decision text, highest signal), session notes (Coco-distilled with Sean quotes in Notable moments), and philosophy/guild-metaphor/CLAUDE files. Transcripts deferred (too costly for marginal signal over session notes). V1 structure: principles-list, matching how Sean articulates taste; structured-by-decision-type deferred. Freshness is docs-maintenance cadence, signaled by override-rate instrument. Volume curation is the central authoring challenge since system prompt is finite context. Future state worth tracking: role instructions produced by a function() rather than a static .md pointer (see related click c-mo3tiwy6).

#### c-mo3tivzf-3c0b6de9406b [concluded]
> When no patron role is configured in Astrolabe, should patron-anima steps be skipped entirely or should the framework ship a default patron?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Skip patron-anima steps when no patron role is configured. No framework default. Rationale: every patron has different taste by definition, so a framework default represents no patron's taste (incoherent, not neutral); a clicks-only default would be too thin without sanctum-authored sources; a quality-degraded default risks poisoning trust in the mechanism. Framework provides the configuration slot; patrons fill it to opt in. Pattern observation worth tracking: this opens adjacent questions about dynamically shaping rigs and prompts when pipeline features are optional.

Status: live

## c-mo3qj676-ed75775ba337 [live]
> Design the BA/PO pre-plan stage — produce a clarified-feature-spec from the brief via extract, extrapolate, and interview; becomes the input contract for the reader-analyst phase
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-18T02:45:49.218Z

### c-mo3qj9by-ed5ad14e41d6 [concluded]
> Define the shape of the clarified-feature-spec — required/optional sections, minimum content per section, field types, and validation rules for 'this spec is complete enough to hand to the analyst'
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Final shape of the clarified-feature-spec. Six sections total.

| Section | Tier | Structure |
|---|---|---|
| Summary | Required | Prose paragraph, ~500 chars, describes what the feature is and who it's for |
| Behaviors | Required (≥1) | List of {id: B1..., trigger, outcome, source} — uniform records regardless of flow type |
| Acceptance Criteria | Required (≥1) | List of {id: AC1..., statement, refs to behaviors} — each behavior must be covered by ≥1 AC |
| Out of Scope | Required-presence | List of explicit non-goals; can be 'nothing notable' but must be addressed |
| Edge Cases | Required-presence | **References-only** — list of {behavior-id, one-line rationale}; classification layer pointing into Behaviors |
| Open Questions | Required-presence | List of {question, context, confidence} or empty; forces BA self-check |

**Sections explicitly dropped from initial shape** (see sibling clicks for rationale):
- Dependencies (c-mo3r9a8s) — requires canon-research / feature catalog infrastructure we don't have
- Success Metrics (c-mo3r9auv) — aspirational; verification umbrella handles this downstream
- Use Cases (c-mo3rhkrh) — fabrication and scope-creep risk; gap filled by AC and Edge Cases

**Cross-cutting rules:**
- **Provenance tagging:** every item in Behaviors and optional-anchored positions must cite source (brief | extrapolated from [anchor] | interview Q[n]). Creates auditability and prevents fabrication.
- **User-observability test:** every outcome in Behaviors must be user-visible (BA domain). Implementation-visible-only changes belong to the analyst, not the BA.
- **Cross-section linking:** AC ids reference behavior ids; Edge Cases entries reference behavior ids. Stable identifiers enable machine-checkable coverage.

**Validation rule for 'complete enough to hand to analyst':**
- All required sections non-empty with real content (not placeholders)
- ≥1 behavior and ≥1 AC
- Every behavior covered by ≥1 AC
- Required-presence sections addressed (can be 'none' but must say so)

**Governing principle** (inherited from the broader BA/PO stage role split): BA resolves product-layer ambiguity; reader/analyst resolves implementation ambiguity. Sections that invite speculation beyond the BA's accessible sources (brief, referenced material, interview) were trimmed.
Created: 2026-04-18T02:45:53.278Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:24:58.263Z

#### c-mo3r9a8s-1d9bb8e02477 [concluded]
> Dependencies section — why excluded from initial shape and when to revisit
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Excluded from initial feature-spec shape. The BA has no reliable source for product-level system knowledge beyond what's explicitly named in the brief. The three sources the BA can reach — brief text, referenced material (concluded clicks/docs the brief links), Patron Anima interview — don't include a queryable feature catalog. The anima is a judgment layer for proposed content, not a retrieval layer for existing content; asking it to enumerate related features invites fabrication from implicit training knowledge.

Revisit when canon-research (c-mo3d6pkh) or atlas (c-mo1mqhql) lands. At that point either (a) canon-research populates Dependencies before BA runs, or (b) BA queries canon-research during its pass. Until then, dependencies named in the brief are visible to the analyst directly; a dedicated section is redundant and risky.
Created: 2026-04-18T03:06:07.516Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:06:14.800Z

#### c-mo3r9auv-7124ed1f0aa7 [concluded]
> Success Metrics section — why excluded from initial shape and when to revisit
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Excluded from initial feature-spec shape. Metrics are inherently aspirational/future-state — the BA has no way to validate them or tie them to real measurement infrastructure. Risk of aspirational fabrication is high: the BA can produce plausible-sounding metrics that nobody tracks, creating false signal for the analyst (implying success criteria beyond Acceptance Criteria) and inviting scope creep.

Revisit after the verification umbrella (c-mo3dj3u3) lands. Verification work will produce real measurement infrastructure and define what 'working in production' looks like structurally. Once that exists, Success Metrics as a feature-spec section becomes grounded in concrete instrumentation rather than speculation.
Created: 2026-04-18T03:06:08.311Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:06:18.174Z

#### c-mo3rhkrh-d6ace5546b49 [concluded]
> Use Cases section — why excluded from initial shape
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Excluded from initial feature-spec shape. Use cases are narrative scenarios describing how behaviors compose into user flows, but in practice they are where BA fabrication is most tempting — every 'what if the user also wants X' is an invitation to invent scope the brief never committed to.

**Risks:**
- Fabricated scenarios that invent user goals not in the brief (high)
- Scope creep via narrative (medium-to-high) — a use case can imply behaviors that aren't in the Behaviors section
- Redundancy with behaviors + summary for simple features (low individual cost but clutter)

**The gap isn't unfilled without this section:**
- Implications like 'cancelled = completion for scheduling' can be captured as Acceptance Criteria ('AC: A cancelled blocker unblocks its dependents') or as Edge Cases
- Briefs often already contain narrative prose; the analyst can read the raw brief directly
- Atomic behaviors plus AC are sufficient for most features

**Precedent alignment:** consistent with dropping Success Metrics and Dependencies — each section that invites BA speculation beyond accessible sources gets trimmed. Simpler BA role, smaller surface for prompts to go off-script.

Revisit only if pattern emerges where analyst consistently misses compositions that atomic behaviors don't make obvious.
Created: 2026-04-18T03:12:34.397Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:12:48.762Z

#### c-mo3rwsju-b7c6c08c3b93 [concluded]
> Edge Cases section design — references-only pattern pointing to behaviors with one-line rationale, required-presence
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Edge Cases section is kept as **required-presence** but contains **only references to behaviors** — not standalone content.

**Structure per entry:** behavior id + one-line rationale (format b).

Example:
```
Edge Cases:
- B3 — cancelled-blocker case (symmetric to completion)
- B5 — simultaneous release of shared blocker
- B7 — malformed blocked_by entry
```

**Rationale for references-only design:**
- Edge cases ARE behaviors with unusual triggers — the distinction is classification, not semantic. Keeping them in Behaviors (uniform records) avoids fuzzy boundaries between sections and eliminates the 'is this a behavior or edge case?' judgment call.
- Tagging each behavior record with a 'flow' field felt awkward; a dedicated classification section is cleaner.
- The rationale per reference makes the section self-contained for human readers without forcing cross-reference; it also documents *why* the BA classified it as edge, which is useful signal.

**Validation rules for edge-case behaviors:**
- Must pass the user-observability test (outcome is user-visible)
- Must be sourced from (1) brief explicit mention, (3) behavior-trigger symmetry extrapolation, or (4) interview confirmation. Not from vague universal-quantifier implications (source 2) or BA invention (source 5).

**Required-presence status** forces the BA to explicitly consider 'any edge cases?' rather than silently skip. Empty list is valid; omission is not.
Created: 2026-04-18T03:24:24.330Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:24:37.599Z

##### c-mo3s410k-e9dd50be7a03 [live]
> Operational criteria for edge-case classification — what test does the BA apply to decide whether a behavior belongs in the Edge Cases section vs being presented as main flow? The structural design is settled (references-only), but 'edge vs main' is currently BA judgment with no codified criterion. Candidate tests: frequency-of-path (rare triggers = edge), casual-reader-expectation (would a reader expect this behavior to exist? if no → edge), symmetry-status (explicitly-named trigger = main, symmetrically-implied trigger = edge).
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-18T03:30:01.892Z

### c-mo3qjahj-1cbdf4fe9e18 [concluded]
> Define the BA role — is it a new anima, a persona the planner adopts, or just an engine prompt? How does it relate to the reader-analyst, and does it persist across stages?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Role is a **new anima**, named **Distiller**, peer of the Patron Anima and the downstream analyst (analyst-anima name TBD).

**Substrate: new anima** (not a persona of the planner, not an engine prompt)
- "Trained on BA practice" implies a curriculum. Engine prompts have system prompts, not curricula — rules out prompt-only.
- Distinct from Patron Anima rules out persona-flip of the planner: the planner is a generalist, the Distiller is a specialist with its own methodology.
- Anima-as-defined (name + role + curriculum + temperament) fits exactly: methodology-as-curriculum, interviewer-as-role, precise-non-presumptuous temperament.

**Peer relationships** — three distinct animas at the pre-plan + plan stages, none a persona of another:
- **Patron Anima** — interviewee; taste-heavy; patron-corpus-trained
- **Distiller** — interviewer; factual-context-heavy; BA-practice-trained
- **Analyst** (name TBD) — downstream; implementation-layer focus

**Pipeline position and persistence**
- Runs once per commission as the pre-plan stage; produces the clarified feature-spec (shape per sibling c-mo3qj9by) from the brief; hands off to the analyst and exits.
- MVP: not re-entered. Re-invocation (e.g., analyst surfaces a product-layer question) is a future option, not initial scope.
- Relation to reader-analyst: upstream; Distiller resolves product-layer ambiguity, analyst resolves implementation-layer. Governing principle inherited from the feature-spec click conclusion.

**Naming rationale** (why Distiller, not BA / PO / Clarifier / Prism / Spec-wright)
- **BA** carries implementation/systems-analysis baggage in many industries; risk an agent reads BA and drifts into implementation territory we're trying to quarantine.
- **PO** collides with Patron Anima — the patron already plays the product-owner role in Nexus.
- **Clarifier** is literal but passive; doesn't evoke a craft.
- **Prism** captures structuring but not eliciting (a prism doesn't interview).
- **Spec-wright** was the runner-up: names the output, pure guild suffix, Nexus-native.
- **Distiller** won on three counts: active craft (captures extract/extrapolate/interview, not just output); self-scoping (substance-layer, not structure-layer — naturally holds the role away from implementation); and pairs cleanly with Patron-as-source in the interviewer-interviewee framing. Workshop metaphor (the distillery / still) becomes useful scaffolding for later role-design discussions about tools and curriculum.

**Transcript note carried forward** (from the session this was concluded in): Distiller and Patron Anima are genuinely distinct roles with distinct needs — Distiller needs factual context (brief, referenced material, structural knowledge of good feature-specs) more than taste; Patron Anima needs taste corpus more than factual lookup. Implies different training sources and different context-injection patterns for the two animas. Sub-subs (c-mo3s293s, c-mo3s2abg, c-mo3s2bo8) operationalize this distinction for the Distiller side.
Created: 2026-04-18T02:45:54.775Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:48:48.481Z

#### c-mo3s293s-b860916025a1 [concluded]
> BA information access — what does the BA see at runtime (prompt context) and what tools/MCP surface does it have for active lookup? Distinct from the Patron Anima's taste corpus; the BA needs factual context (brief, referenced material, concluded clicks) more than taste.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: **Governing principle:** the Distiller doesn't explore. It reads what's given, interviews when gaps appear, extrapolates from named anchors, and emits. No search, no wandering — if something isn't in the brief or its references, the Distiller asks the Patron Anima.

**Static context**

*System prompt (Distiller role instructions — stable across invocations):*
- Role identity, temperament, governing principles (product-layer only, provenance required, no exploration, no fabrication)
- Feature-spec schema (six sections, validation rules, cross-section linking rules — from sibling c-mo3qj9by)
- BA methodology — extract / extrapolate / interview ordering and criteria (details in c-mo3qjbmw)
- Interview technique — non-leading questions, detecting hand-waving (details in c-mo3qjcuw)
- Exemplar specs showing what "good" reads like (handful of worked examples embed

content: c-mo3u8ay0-51a6bf98056d definitely got put in the wrong place

content.text:
Looking at c-mo3d6lyd after all the evolution:

## The shape

c-mo3d6lyd has 10 children now. Grouping them by the category each addresses:

- **Evidence gathering** — canon-research, external-research
- **Deliberation** — experts panel, steelman+arbiter, Patron Anima
- **Meta / routing / scoring** — ambiguity score, cost-weighted planning, arbitrary-choice mechanism
- **Autonomy** — Bold Agent / boldness budget
- **One misfit** — Distiller (the one I just moved here)

The first nine fit the parent's framing cleanly: "evidence-based decisional machinery for the planner — let the system speak for the patron via research, experts panel, steelman/arbiter, Patron Anima, and boldness mechanisms." They're all *techniques the planner uses* to make better decisions.

The Distiller doesn't fit that frame. It's not a planner decision mechanism — it's a **pre-plan pipeline stage** that transforms a messy brief into a structured feature-spec *before* the planner runs. It uses Patron Anima via ask-patron, but so does every other animal-interaction tool; that doesn't make it decisional machinery.

## Recommendation — move Distiller to the fidelity umbrella

Reparent c-mo3qj676 from c-mo3d6lyd to **c-mo38bgoh** ("Establish brief→planner fidelity — prevent the planner from drifting from the brief").

Rationale:
- Distiller's core purpose *is* brief→planner fidelity — producing a machine-parseable spec so the planner can faithfully act on patron intent.
- c-mo38bgoh's existing children are *reactive* fidelity mechanisms (fidelity audit, contradiction detection, phase passes). The Distiller is a *proactive* fidelity intervention — change the input to the planner so ambiguity gets resolved at the product layer before planning starts.
- They naturally coexist: Distiller reduces ambiguity going in; audit catches drift coming out.

Alternative I considered and rejected: promote Distiller to its own top-level planner-quality umbrella. Tempting because it's a distinct pipeline stage with 12+ descendants, but the existing four umbrellas are organized by *problem space* (fidelity, decisional machinery, scope, verification), not by *pipeline stage*. The Distiller solves a fidelity problem via a new stage; the stage-ness doesn't justify breaking the umbrella pattern. If it grows to warrant its own umbrella later, promoting it is cheap.

## Adjacent observation (not reparenting, but related)

With the Distiller moved out, **c-mo3o292o** (Patron Anima pipeline-position) has been hollowed out:
- One child dropped (c-mo3phvuw — Socratic interview reshaped into Distiller)
- One child parked (c-mo3phwj6 — mid-plan consultation)
- The BA/PO subtree that was its third load-bearing child moved to sibling decisional-machinery

The remaining question "pre-decision, post-decision, or both" has effectively been answered by the surrounding work: Patron Anima appears pre-plan via Distiller interview, and post-decision via verdict/override. Mid-plan consultation stays parked. That click is a candidate for conclusion, but it's a click-closure question, not a reparenting one — I'd want your nod before closing.

## Other subtrees

The Patron Anima scoping tree (c-mo3d6trd → c-mo3o0euk) is coherent and still making progress. No reorganization needed.

The other 9 children of c-mo3d6lyd all fit the decisional-machinery theme cleanly. No restructuring warranted.

Want me to do the Distiller reparent (and optionally close c-mo3o292o)?
